**Counterinsurgency** (**COIN**, or [NATO](/wiki/NATO "NATO") spelling **counter-insurgency**[[1]](#cite_note-1)) is "the totality of actions aimed at defeating [irregular forces](/wiki/Irregular_military "Irregular military")".[[2]](#cite_note-2) The [Oxford English Dictionary](/wiki/Oxford_English_Dictionary "Oxford English Dictionary") defines counterinsurgency as any "military or political action taken against the activities of guerrillas or revolutionaries"[[3]](#cite_note-3) and can be considered war by a state against a [non-state adversary](/wiki/Non-state_actor "Non-state actor").[[4]](#cite_note-Sheldon-4) [Insurgency](/wiki/Insurgency "Insurgency") and counterinsurgency campaigns have been waged since [ancient history](/wiki/Ancient_history "Ancient history"). Western thought on fighting 'small wars' gained interest during initial periods of [European colonisation](/wiki/European_colonisation "European colonisation"),[[5]](#cite_note-:1-5) while modern thinking on counterinsurgency was developed during [decolonization](/wiki/Decolonization "Decolonization").[[4]](#cite_note-Sheldon-4)

During insurgency and counterinsurgency, the distinction between [civilians](/wiki/Civilian "Civilian") and [combatants](/wiki/Combatant "Combatant") is often blurred.[[6]](#cite_note-6) Counterinsurgency may involve attempting to [win the hearts and minds](/wiki/Winning_hearts_and_minds "Winning hearts and minds") of populations supporting the insurgency.[[7]](#cite_note-7)[[8]](#cite_note-8) Alternatively, it may be waged in an attempt to intimidate[[4]](#cite_note-Sheldon-4)[[9]](#cite_note-9) or eliminate civilian populations suspected of loyalty to the insurgency through indiscriminate violence.[[4]](#cite_note-Sheldon-4)[[10]](#cite_note-10)

## Models

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Counterinsurgency&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Models")]

> The guerrilla must swim in the people as the fish swims in the sea.

–Aphorism based on the writing of [Mao Zedong](/wiki/Mao_Zedong "Mao Zedong")[[11]](#cite_note-mao-11)

Counterinsurgency is normally conducted as a combination of conventional military operations and other means, such as [demoralization](/wiki/Demoralization_(warfare) "Demoralization (warfare)") in the form of [propaganda](/wiki/Propaganda "Propaganda"), [Psy-ops](/wiki/Psy-ops "Psy-ops"), and [assassinations](/wiki/Assassination "Assassination"). Counter-insurgency operations include many different facets: [military](/wiki/Military "Military"), [paramilitary](/wiki/Paramilitary "Paramilitary"), [political](/wiki/Political "Political"), [economic](/wiki/Economic "Economic"), [psychological](/wiki/Psychological "Psychological"), and civic actions taken to defeat [insurgency](/wiki/Insurgency "Insurgency").[[12]](#cite_note-12)

To understand counterinsurgency, one must understand [insurgency](/wiki/Insurgency#Political_rhetoric,_myths,_and_models "Insurgency") to comprehend the dynamics of revolutionary warfare. Counter-insurgency focuses on bridging these gaps. Insurgents take advantage of social issues known as gaps. When the gaps are wide, they create a sea of discontent, creating the environment in which the insurgent can operate.[[13]](#cite_note-Eizenstat-2005-13)

In *The Insurgent Archipelago*, John Mackinlay puts forward the concept of an evolution of the insurgency from the [Maoist](/wiki/Maoism "Maoism") paradigm of the golden age of insurgency to the global insurgency of the start of the 21st century. He defines this distinction as "Maoist" and "post-Maoist" insurgency.[[14]](#cite_note-14)[*[page needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*]

## Counterinsurgency theorists

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Counterinsurgency&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Counterinsurgency theorists")]

### Santa Cruz de Marcenado

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Counterinsurgency&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Santa Cruz de Marcenado")]

The third Marques of [Santa Cruz de Marcenado](/wiki/Santa_Cruz_de_Marcenado "Santa Cruz de Marcenado") (1684–1732) is an early author who wrote both advise for conventional military matters he also gave suggestions on the topic of "rebellions".[[5]](#cite_note-:1-5) In his *Reflexiones Militares*, published between 1726 and 1730, he discussed how to spot early signs of an incipient insurgency, prevent insurgencies,[[5]](#cite_note-:1-5) and counter them, if they could not be warded off. Strikingly, Santa Cruz recognized that insurgencies are usually due to real grievances: "A state rarely rises up without the fault of its governors."[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Consequently, he advocated clemency towards the population and good governance, to seek the people's "heart and love".[[15]](#cite_note-test-15)

### Thomas-Robert Bugeaud

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Counterinsurgency&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Thomas-Robert Bugeaud")]

General [Bugeaud](/wiki/Thomas_Robert_Bugeaud "Thomas Robert Bugeaud") served extensively in the [French war of conquest in Algeria](/wiki/French_conquest_of_Algeria "French conquest of Algeria") and was also personally present (although as a junior officer) during the [Peninsular War](/wiki/Peninsular_War#Iberia_in_revolt "Peninsular War"). After he returned to France he wrote extensively about Algeria and has been cited as an early commander whose conduct contains many aspects of modern counterinsurgency doctrine, such as intelligence lead operations[[5]](#cite_note-:1-5) in combination with [irregular warfare](/wiki/Irregular_warfare "Irregular warfare") and [psychological warfare](/wiki/Psychological_warfare "Psychological warfare").[[16]](#cite_note-:2-16) However in comparison to theorists and practitioners who would come after him Bugeaud's methods have been analysed as being more "enemy centric" - favouring punitive raids or [razzias](/wiki/Razzia_(military) "Razzia (military)") conducted by light mobile forces[[16]](#cite_note-:2-16) rather than the more "population centric" of both modern doctrines and also latter French Generals during the colonial period who developed the [oil spot](#Oil_spot) method.[[5]](#cite_note-:1-5) Bugeaud's approach was also less discriminatory, between civilian targets and military with razzias often targeting by design subsistence agriculture that supported groups that had not yet acquiesced to French authority.

### B. H. Liddell Hart

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Counterinsurgency&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: B. H. Liddell Hart")]

[Liddell Hart](/wiki/Liddell_Hart "Liddell Hart") attributed the failure of counterinsurgencies to various causes. First, as pointed out in the Insurgency addendum to the second version of his book *Strategy: The Indirect Approach*, a popular insurgency has an inherent advantage over any occupying force. He showed as a prime example the [French occupation of Spain](/wiki/Peninsular_War "Peninsular War") during the [Napoleonic Wars](/wiki/Napoleonic_Wars "Napoleonic Wars"). Whenever Spanish forces managed to constitute themselves into a regular fighting force, the superior French forces beat them in the vast majority engagements.[[17]](#cite_note-17)

However, once dispersed and decentralized, the [irregular nature](/wiki/Irregular_warfare "Irregular warfare") of the rebel campaigns proved a decisive counter to French superiority on the battlefield. [Napoleon](/wiki/Napoleon "Napoleon")'s army had no means of effectively combating the rebels, and in the end, their strength and morale were so sapped that when [Wellington](/wiki/Arthur_Wellesley,_1st_Duke_of_Wellington "Arthur Wellesley, 1st Duke of Wellington") was finally able to challenge French forces in the field, the French had almost no choice but to abandon the situation.[[18]](#cite_note-18)

Counterinsurgency efforts may be successful, especially when the insurgents are unpopular. The [Philippine–American War](/wiki/Philippine%E2%80%93American_War "Philippine–American War"),[[19]](#cite_note-19) the [Shining Path](/wiki/Shining_Path "Shining Path") in Peru, and the [Malayan Emergency](/wiki/Malayan_Emergency "Malayan Emergency")[[20]](#cite_note-20) have been the sites of failed insurgencies.

Hart also points to the experiences of [T. E. Lawrence](/wiki/T._E._Lawrence "T. E. Lawrence") and the [Arab Revolt](/wiki/Arab_Revolt "Arab Revolt") during [World War I](/wiki/World_War_I "World War I") as another example of the power of the rebel/insurgent. Though the [Ottomans](/wiki/Ottoman_Turks "Ottoman Turks") often had advantages in manpower of more than 100 to 1, the [Arabs](/wiki/Arab "Arab")' ability to materialize out of the desert, strike, and disappear again often left the [Turks](/wiki/Ottoman_Empire "Ottoman Empire") reeling and paralyzed, creating an opportunity for regular [British forces](/wiki/British_forces "British forces") to sweep in and finish the Turkish forces off.[[21]](#cite_note-21)

In both the preceding cases, the insurgents and rebel fighters were working in conjunction with or in a manner complementary to regular forces. Such was also the case with the [French Resistance](/wiki/French_Resistance "French Resistance") during [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II") and the [National Liberation Front](/wiki/Viet_Cong "Viet Cong") during the [Vietnam War](/wiki/Vietnam_War "Vietnam War"). The strategy in these cases is for the irregular combatant to weaken and destabilize the enemy to such a degree that victory is easy or assured for the regular forces.

According to Liddell Hart, there are few effective counter-measures to this strategy. So long as the insurgency maintains popular support, it will retain all of its strategic advantages of mobility, invisibility, and legitimacy in its own eyes and the eyes of the people. So long as this is the situation, an insurgency essentially cannot be defeated by regular forces.[[22]](#cite_note-22)

### David Galula

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Counterinsurgency&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: David Galula")]

[David Galula](/wiki/David_Galula "David Galula") gained his practical experience in counterinsurgency as a [French Army](/wiki/French_Army "French Army") officer in the [Algerian War](/wiki/Algerian_War "Algerian War"). His theory of counterinsurgency is not primarily military, but a combination of military, political and social actions under the strong control of a single authority.

Galula proposes four "laws" for counterinsurgency:[[23]](#cite_note-23)

1. The aim of the war is to gain the support of the population rather than control of territory.
2. Most of the population will be neutral in the conflict; support of the masses can be obtained with the help of an active friendly minority.
3. Support of the population may be lost. The population must be efficiently protected to allow it to cooperate without fear of retribution by the opposite party.
4. Order enforcement should be done progressively by removing or driving away armed opponents, then gaining the support of the population, and eventually strengthening positions by building infrastructure and setting long-term relationships with the population. This must be done area by area, using a pacified territory as a basis of operation to conquer a neighboring area.

Galula contends that:

> A victory [in a counterinsurgency] is not the destruction in a given area of the insurgent's forces and his political organization. ... A victory is that plus the permanent isolation of the insurgent from the population, isolation not enforced upon the population, but maintained by and with the population. ... In conventional warfare, strength is assessed according to military or other tangible criteria, such as the number of divisions, the position they hold, the industrial resources, etc. In revolutionary warfare, strength must be assessed by the extent of support from the population as measured in terms of political organization at the grassroots. The counterinsurgent reaches a position of strength when his power is embedded in a political organization issuing from, and firmly supported by, the population.[[24]](#cite_note-galula-24)

With his four principles in mind, Galula goes on to describe a general military and political strategy to put them into operation in an area that is under full insurgent control:

> In a Selected Area
>
> 1. Concentrate enough armed forces to destroy or to expel the main body of armed insurgents.  
> 2. Detach for the area sufficient troops to oppose an insurgent come back in strength, install these troops in the hamlets, villages, and towns where the population lives.  
> 3. Establish contact with the population, control its movements in order to cut off its links with the guerrillas.  
> 4. Destroy the local insurgent political organization.  
> 5. Set up, by means of elections, new provisional local authorities.  
> 6. Test those authorities by assigning them various concrete tasks. Replace the softs and the incompetents, give full support to the active leaders. Organize self-defense units.  
> 7. Group and educate the leaders in a national political movement.
>
> 8. Win over or suppress the last insurgent remnants.[[24]](#cite_note-galula-24)

According to Galula, some of these steps can be skipped in areas that are only partially under insurgent control, and most of them are unnecessary in areas already controlled by the government.[[24]](#cite_note-galula-24) Thus the essence of counterinsurgency warfare is summed up by Galula as "Build (or rebuild) a political machine from the population upward."[[25]](#cite_note-25)

### Robert Thompson

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Counterinsurgency&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Robert Thompson")]

[Robert Grainger Ker Thompson](/wiki/Robert_Grainger_Ker_Thompson "Robert Grainger Ker Thompson") wrote *Defeating Communist Insurgency*[[26]](#cite_note-26) in 1966, wherein he argued that a successful counterinsurgency effort must be proactive in seizing the initiative from insurgents. Thompson outlines five basic principles for a successful counterinsurgency:

1. The government must have a clear political aim: to establish and maintain a free, independent and united country that is politically and economically stable and viable;
2. The government must function in accordance with the law;
3. The government must have an overall plan;
4. The government must give priority to defeating political subversion, not the guerrilla fighters;
5. In the guerrilla phase of an insurgency, a government must secure its base areas first.[[27]](#cite_note-Hamilton1998-27)[[28]](#cite_note-28)

### David Kilcullen

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Counterinsurgency&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: David Kilcullen")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ab/Kilcullen3Pillars.svg/500px-Kilcullen3Pillars.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Kilcullen3Pillars.svg)

Three Pillars of Counterinsurgency

In "The Three Pillars of Counterinsurgency", Dr. [David Kilcullen](/wiki/David_Kilcullen "David Kilcullen"), the Chief Strategist of the Office of the Coordinator for Counterterrorism of the [U.S. State Department](/wiki/U.S._State_Department "U.S. State Department") in 2006, described a framework for interagency cooperation in counterinsurgency operations. His pillars – Security, Political and Economic – support the overarching goal of Control, but are based on Information:

> This is because perception is crucial in developing control and influence over population groups. Substantive security, political and economic measures are critical but to be effective they must rest upon, and integrate with a broader information strategy. Every action in counterinsurgency sends a message; the purpose of the information campaign is to consolidate and unify this message. ... Importantly, the information campaign has to be conducted at a global, regional and local level — because modern insurgents draw upon global networks of sympathy, support, funding and recruitment.[[29]](#cite_note-kilcullen-29)

Kilcullen considers the three pillars to be of equal importance because

> unless they are developed in parallel, the campaign becomes unbalanced: too much economic assistance with inadequate security, for example, simply creates an array of soft targets for the insurgents. Similarly, too much security assistance without political consensus or governance simply creates more capable armed groups. In developing each pillar, we measure progress by gauging effectiveness (capability and capacity) and legitimacy (the degree to which the population accepts that government actions are in its interest).[[29]](#cite_note-kilcullen-29)

The overall goal, according to this model, "is not to reduce violence to zero or to kill every insurgent, but rather to return the overall system to normality — noting that 'normality' in one society may look different from normality in another. In each case, we seek not only to establish control, but also to consolidate that control and then transfer it to permanent, effective, and legitimate institutions."[[29]](#cite_note-kilcullen-29)[[30]](#cite_note-30)

### Martin van Creveld

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Counterinsurgency&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Martin van Creveld")]

Military historian [Martin van Creveld](/wiki/Martin_van_Creveld "Martin van Creveld"), noting that almost all attempts to deal with insurgency have ended in failure, advises:

> The first, and absolutely indispensable, thing to do is throw overboard 99 percent of the literature on counterinsurgency, counter guerrilla, counterterrorism, and the like. Since most of it was written by the losing side, it is of little value.[[31]](#cite_note-31)

In examining why so many counterinsurgencies by powerful militaries fail against weaker enemies, Van Creveld identifies a key dynamic that he illustrates by the metaphor of killing a child. Regardless of whether the child started the fight or how well armed the child is, an adult in a fight with a child will feel that he is acting unjustly if he harms the child and foolish if the child harms him; he will, therefore, wonder if the fight is necessary.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Van Creveld argues that "by definition, a strong counterinsurgent who uses his strength to kill the members of a small, weak organization of insurgents – let alone the civilian population by which it is surrounded, and which may lend it support – will commit crimes in an unjust cause," while "a child who is in a serious fight with an adult is justified in using every and any means available – not because he or she is right, but because he or she has no choice".[[32]](#cite_note-32) Every act of insurgency becomes, from the perspective of the counterinsurgent, a reason to end the conflict, while also being a reason for the insurgents to continue until victory. [Trường Chinh](/wiki/Tr%C6%B0%E1%BB%9Dng_Chinh "Trường Chinh"), second in command to [Ho Chi Minh](/wiki/Ho_Chi_Minh "Ho Chi Minh") of [Vietnam](/wiki/Vietnam "Vietnam"), wrote in his *Primer for Revolt*:

> The guiding principle of the strategy for our whole resistance must be to prolong the war. To protract the war is the key to victory. Why must the war be protracted? ... If we throw the whole of our forces into a few battles to try to decide the outcome, we shall certainly be defeated and the enemy will win. On the other hand, if while fighting we maintain our forces, expand them, train our army and people, learn military tactics … and at the same time wear down the enemy forces, we shall weary and discourage them in such a way that, strong as they are, they will become weak and will meet defeat instead of victory.[[33]](#cite_note-33)

Van Creveld thus identifies "time" as the key factor in counterinsurgency. In an attempt to find lessons from the few cases of successful counterinsurgency, of which he lists two clear cases: the British efforts during [The Troubles](/wiki/The_Troubles "The Troubles") of [Northern Ireland](/wiki/Northern_Ireland "Northern Ireland") and the 1982 [Hama massacre](/wiki/1982_Hama_massacre "1982 Hama massacre") carried out by the [Syrian](/wiki/Syria "Syria") government to suppress the [Muslim Brotherhood](/wiki/Muslim_Brotherhood "Muslim Brotherhood"), he asserts that the "core of the difficulty is neither military nor political, but moral" and outlines two distinct methods.[[34]](#cite_note-34)

The first method relies on superb intelligence, provided by those who know the natural and artificial environment of the conflict as well as the insurgents. Once such superior intelligence is gained, the counterinsurgents must be trained to a point of high professionalism and discipline such that they will exercise discrimination and restraint. Through such discrimination and restraint, the counterinsurgents do not alienate members of the populace besides those already fighting them, while delaying the time when the counterinsurgents become disgusted by their own actions and demoralized.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

General Patrick Walters, the British commander of troops in Northern Ireland, explicitly stated that his objective was not to kill as many terrorists as possible but to ensure that as few people on both sides were killed. In the vast majority of counterinsurgencies, the "forces of order" kill far more people than they lose. In contrast and using very rough figures, the struggle in Northern Ireland had cost the United Kingdom three thousand fatal casualties. Of the three thousand, about seventeen hundred were civilians...of the remaining, a thousand were British soldiers. No more than three hundred were terrorists, a ratio of three to one.[[35]](#cite_note-van_Creveld,_235-35)

If the prerequisites for the first method – excellent intelligence, superbly trained and disciplined soldiers and police, and an iron will to avoid being provoked into lashing out – are lacking, van Creveld posits that counterinsurgents who still want to win must use the second method exemplified by the [Hama massacre](/wiki/1982_Hama_massacre "1982 Hama massacre"). In 1982, the regime of Syrian president [Hafez al-Assad](/wiki/Hafez_al-Assad "Hafez al-Assad") was on the point of being overwhelmed by the countrywide insurgency of the [Muslim Brotherhood](/wiki/Muslim_Brotherhood_in_Syria "Muslim Brotherhood in Syria"). Al-Assad sent a [Syrian Army](/wiki/Syrian_Army "Syrian Army") division under his brother [Rifaat](/wiki/Rifaat_al-Assad "Rifaat al-Assad") to the city of [Hama](/wiki/Hama "Hama"), known to be the center of the resistance.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Following a counterattack by the Brotherhood, Rifaat used his heavy artillery to demolish the city, killing between 10-25,000 people, including many women and children. Asked by reporters what had happened, Hafez al-Assad exaggerated the damage and deaths, promoted the commanders who carried out the attacks, and razed Hama's well-known great mosque, replacing it with a parking lot. With the Muslim Brotherhood scattered, the population was so cowed that it would be years before opposition groups dared to disobey the regime again and, van Creveld argues, the massacre most likely saved the regime and prevented a bloody [civil war](/wiki/Civil_war "Civil war").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Van Creveld condenses al-Assad's strategy into five rules while noting that they could easily have been written by [Niccolò Machiavelli](/wiki/Niccol%C3%B2_Machiavelli "Niccolò Machiavelli"):[[35]](#cite_note-van_Creveld,_235-35)

1. There are situations in which cruelty is necessary, and refusing to apply necessary cruelty is a betrayal of the people who put you into power. When pressed to cruelty, never threaten your opponent but disguise your intention and feign weakness until you strike.
2. Once you decide to strike, it is better to kill too many than not enough. If another strike is needed, it reduces the impact of the first strike. Repeated strikes will also endanger the morale of the counterinsurgent troops; soldiers forced to commit repeated atrocities will likely begin to resort to alcohol or drugs to force themselves to carry out orders and will inevitably lose their military edge, eventually turning into a danger to their commanders.
3. Act as soon as possible. More lives will be saved by decisive action early, than by prolonging the insurgency. The longer you wait, the more inured the population will be to bloodshed, and the more barbaric your action will have to be to make an impression.
4. Strike openly. Do not apologize, make excuses about "[collateral damage](/wiki/Collateral_damage "Collateral damage")", express regret, or promise investigations. Afterwards, make sure that as many people as possible know of your strike; the media is useful for this purpose, but be careful not to let them interview survivors and arouse sympathy.
5. Do not command the strike yourself, in case it doesn't work for some reason and you need to disown your commander and try another strategy. If it does work, present your commander to the world, explain what you have done and make certain that everyone understands that you are ready to strike again.[[36]](#cite_note-36)

### Lorenzo Zambernardi

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Counterinsurgency&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Lorenzo Zambernardi")]

In "Counterinsurgency's Impossible Trilemma", Dr. Lorenzo Zambernardi, an Italian academic now working in the United States, clarifies the tradeoffs involved in counterinsurgency operations.[[37]](#cite_note-37) He argues that counterinsurgency involves three main goals, but in real practice, a counterinsurgent needs to choose two goals out of three. Relying on [economic theory](/wiki/Economics "Economics"), this is what Zambernardi labels the "impossible trilemma" of counterinsurgency. Specifically, the impossible trilemma suggests that it is impossible to simultaneously achieve: 1) force protection, 2) distinction between enemy combatants and non-combatants, and 3) the physical elimination of insurgents.[[38]](#cite_note-38)

According to Zambernardi, in pursuing any two of these three goals, a state must forgo some portion of the third objective. In particular, a state can protect its armed forces while destroying insurgents, but only by indiscriminately killing civilians as the [Ottomans](/wiki/Ottoman_Empire "Ottoman Empire"), [Italians](/wiki/Italy "Italy"), and [Nazis](/wiki/Nazi_Germany "Nazi Germany") did in the Balkans, Libya, and Eastern Europe. It can choose to protect civilians along with its own armed forces instead, avoiding so-called collateral damage, but only by abandoning the objective of destroying the insurgents. Finally, a state can discriminate between combatants and non-combatants while killing insurgents, but only by increasing the risks for its own troops, because often insurgents will hide behind civilians, or appear to be civilians. So a country must choose two out of three goals and develop a strategy that can successfully accomplish them while sacrificing the third objective.

Zambernardi's theory posits that to protect populations, which is necessary to defeat insurgencies and to physically destroy an insurgency, the counterinsurgent's military forces must be sacrificed, risking the loss of domestic political support.[[39]](#cite_note-39)

### Akali Omeni

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Counterinsurgency&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Akali Omeni")]

Another writer who explores a trio of features relevant to understanding counterinsurgency is Akali Omeni. Within the contemporary context, COIN warfare by African militaries tends to be at the margins of the theoretical debate – even though Africa today is faced with a number of deadly insurgencies. In *Counter-insurgency in Nigeria*, Omeni, a Nigerian academic, discusses the interactions between certain features away from the battlefield, which account for battlefield performance against insurgent warfare. Specifically, Omeni argues that the trio of historical experience, organisational culture (OC) and doctrine, help explain the institution of COIN within militaries and their tendency to reject the innovation and adaptation often necessary to defeat insurgency. These three features, furthermore, influence and can undermine the operational tactics and concepts adopted against insurgents. The COIN challenge, therefore, is not just operational; it also is cultural and institutional before ever it reflects on the battlefield.[[40]](#cite_note-40)

According to Omeni, institutional isomorphism is a sociological phenomenon that constrains the habits of a military (in this case, the Nigerian military) to the long-established, yet increasingly ineffective, ideology of the offensive in irregular warfare. As Omeni writes,

> Whereas the Nigerian military's performance against militias in the Niger Delta already suggested the military had a poor grasp of the threat of insurgent warfare; it was further along the line, as the military struggled against Boko Haram's threat, that the extent of this weakness was exposed. At best, the utility of force, for the Nigerian military, had become but a temporary solution against the threat of insurgent warfare. At worst, the existing model has been perpetuated at such high cost, that urgent revisionist thinking around the idea of counterinsurgency within the military institution may now be required. Additionally, the military's decisive civil war victory, the pivot in Nigeria's strategic culture towards a regional role, and the institutional delegitimization brought about by decades of coups and political meddling, meant that much time went by without substantive revisionism to the military's thinking around its internal function. Change moreover, where it occurred, was institutionally isomorphic and not as far removed from the military's own origins as the intervening decades may have suggested.[[41]](#cite_note-41)

Further, the infantry-centric nature of the [Nigerian Army](/wiki/Nigerian_Army "Nigerian Army")'s battalions, traceable back to the [Nigerian Civil War](/wiki/Nigerian_Civil_War "Nigerian Civil War") back in the 1960s, is reflected in the kinetic nature of the Army's contemporary COIN approach.[[42]](#cite_note-42) This approach has failed to defeat Boko Haram in the way many expected. Certainly, therefore, the popular argument today, which holds that the Nigerian Army has struggled in COIN due to capabilities shortcomings, holds some merit. However, a full-spectrum analysis of the Nigeria case suggests that this popular dominant narrative scarcely scratches the surface of the true COIN challenge. This population-centered challenge, moreover, is one that militaries across the world continue to contend with. And in attempting to solve the COIN puzzle, state forces over the decades have tried a range of tactics.[[43]](#cite_note-43)

### Information-centric theory

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Counterinsurgency&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Information-centric theory")]

Starting in the early 2000s, micro-level data has transformed the analysis of effective counterinsurgency (COIN) operations. Leading this work is the "information-centric" group of theorists and researchers, led by the work of the Empirical Studies of Conflict (ESOC) group at [Princeton University](/wiki/Princeton_University "Princeton University"),[[44]](#cite_note-44) and the Conflict and Peace, Research and Development (CPRD) group at the [University of Michigan](/wiki/University_of_Michigan "University of Michigan").[[45]](#cite_note-45) Berman, Shapiro, and Felter have outlined the modern information-centric model.[[46]](#cite_note-46) In this framework, the critical determinant of counterinsurgent success is information about insurgents provided to counterinsurgents, such as insurgent locations, plans, and targets. Information can be acquired from civilian sources (human intelligence, [HUMINT](/wiki/Human_intelligence_(intelligence_gathering) "Human intelligence (intelligence gathering)")), or through signal intelligence ([SIGINT](/wiki/Signal_intelligence "Signal intelligence")).

### Jeffrey Treistman

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Counterinsurgency&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Jeffrey Treistman")]

Dr. Jeffrey Treistman previously served with the [US Department of State](/wiki/United_States_Department_of_State "United States Department of State") as the Policy Advisor to the Deputy Prime Minister of Iraq, [Salam al-Zaubai](/wiki/Salam_al-Zaubai "Salam al-Zaubai"). In his book, *When Bad States Win: Rethinking Counterinsurgency Strategy*,[[47]](#cite_note-47) he found little evidence to support the ‘hearts and minds’ approach to counterinsurgency. “There is little robust generalizable evidence that population-centric approaches are effective,” he argued.[[48]](#cite_note-48)

In contrast to other COIN theorists, Treistman examined how the gross violation of international law and human rights affected war outcomes. He developed a theory of barbarism as a counterinsurgency strategy — the indiscriminate use of violence against civilians to defeat insurgents. He proposed that under certain conditions, genocide, rape, torture, repression, and other human rights violations could succeed in suppressing a rebellion. Treistman theorized that moderate levels of violence against civilians would not be effective in dissuading insurgents. But if counterinsurgents increased their lethality, cruelty, and violence against civilians it would successfully deter insurgents. “The overwhelming and brutal application of force against civilians,” he argued, “will squash domestic opposition.”[[49]](#cite_note-49) Treistman thus proposed a convex function to model the relationship between barbarism and counterinsurgency outcomes. Moderate levels of repression are insufficient and counterproductive, but as a counterinsurgent increases the degree of lethality and repression it is more likely to defeat insurgents. “Widespread indiscriminate violence and political repression increase the odds of a government victory. As a result, bad states may sometimes defeat insurgencies.”[[50]](#cite_note-50)

## Tactics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Counterinsurgency&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Tactics")]

### "Drain the sea" tactic

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Counterinsurgency&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: \"Drain the sea\" tactic")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/db/German_soldiers_relax_after_destroying_a_village_in_Epirus%2C_Greece.jpg/250px-German_soldiers_relax_after_destroying_a_village_in_Epirus%2C_Greece.jpg)](/wiki/File:German_soldiers_relax_after_destroying_a_village_in_Epirus,_Greece.jpg)

German soldiers relax after destroying a village in Epirus, Greece (1942 or 1943).[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

With regard to tactics, the terms "drain the sea" or "drain the water" involves the forced [relocation](/wiki/Population_transfer "Population transfer") or elimination of the civilian population ("water") to expose the rebels or insurgents ("fish"). In other words, relocation deprives the aforementioned of the support, cover, and resources of the local population. This is typically targeted in that it specifically targets the demographic that supports the insurgency in a limited area where insurgency is taking place, but is indiscriminate from an individual perspective.[[51]](#cite_note-Plakoudas2016-51) Examples of use of this technique in counterinsurgency include [Bar Kokhba revolt](/wiki/Bar_Kokhba_revolt "Bar Kokhba revolt"),[[52]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESheldon2020942-52) [Second Anglo-Boer War](/wiki/Second_Anglo-Boer_War "Second Anglo-Boer War"),[[53]](#cite_note-Downes2007-53) [Greek Civil War](/wiki/Greek_Civil_War "Greek Civil War"),[[51]](#cite_note-Plakoudas2016-51) [General Order No. 11 (1863)](/wiki/General_Order_No._11_(1863) "General Order No. 11 (1863)") in the [American Civil War](/wiki/American_Civil_War "American Civil War"),[[54]](#cite_note-civilwarstlouis.com-54) [Rohingya conflict](/wiki/Rohingya_conflict "Rohingya conflict"),[[55]](#cite_note-Fair2018-55) and [Xinjiang conflict](/wiki/Xinjiang_conflict "Xinjiang conflict").[[56]](#cite_note-56)

"Draining the sea" can also be accomplished through [genocide](/wiki/Genocide "Genocide") by killing the population blamed for the insurgency. During [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II"), Nazi Germany's counterinsurgency (*[Bandenbekämpfung](/wiki/Bandenbek%C3%A4mpfung "Bandenbekämpfung")*, lit. 'bandit fighting') became intertwined with the [Final Solution](/wiki/Final_Solution "Final Solution").[[57]](#cite_note-Pieper2014-57) Indiscriminate violence also has a deterrent effect. [Edward Luttwak](/wiki/Edward_Luttwak "Edward Luttwak") stated, "A massacre once in a while remained an effective warning for decades."[[58]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESheldon2020944-58)

A downside of such methods of counterinsurgency is their severity may provoke increased resistance from the targeted population.[[57]](#cite_note-Pieper2014-57)[[59]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESheldon2020945-59) In contemporary times, concerns about [public opinion](/wiki/Public_opinion "Public opinion") and [international law](/wiki/International_law "International law") can rule out counterinsurgency campaigns using indiscriminate violence.[[59]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESheldon2020945-59)

### Assassination of leaders

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Counterinsurgency&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Assassination of leaders")]

Main articles: [decapitation strike](/wiki/Decapitation_strike "Decapitation strike") and [targeted killing](/wiki/Targeted_killing "Targeted killing")

Assassination of leaders can be a successful counterinsurgency tactic.[[60]](#cite_note-Johnston2012-60)[[61]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESheldon2020940-61)

### Oil spot

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Counterinsurgency&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Oil spot")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/82/Gvnhamlet.jpg/330px-Gvnhamlet.jpg)](/wiki/File:Gvnhamlet.jpg)

A [strategic hamlet](/wiki/Strategic_hamlet "Strategic hamlet") in [South Vietnam](/wiki/South_Vietnam "South Vietnam") c.1964

The oil spot approach is the concentration of counterinsurgent forces into an expanding, secured zone. The origins of the expression is to be found in its initial use by Marshal [Hubert Lyautey](/wiki/Hubert_Lyautey "Hubert Lyautey"), the main theoretician of French colonial warfare and counterinsurgency strategy.[[62]](#cite_note-62)[[63]](#cite_note-porch-63) The oil spot approach was later one of the justifications given in the [Pentagon Papers](/wiki/Pentagon_Papers "Pentagon Papers")[[64]](#cite_note-64) for the [Strategic Hamlet Program](/wiki/Strategic_Hamlet_Program "Strategic Hamlet Program").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### Cordon and search

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Counterinsurgency&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Cordon and search")]

[Cordon and search](/wiki/Cordon_and_search "Cordon and search") is a military tactic, one of the basic counterinsurgency operations[[65]](#cite_note-65) in which an area is cordoned off and premises are searched for weapons or insurgents.[[66]](#cite_note-66)[[67]](#cite_note-67) Other related operations are "Cordon and knock"[[68]](#cite_note-68)[[69]](#cite_note-69) and "Cordon and kick". "Cordon and search" is part of a new doctrine called [Stability and Support Operations](/wiki/Stability_and_Support_Operations "Stability and Support Operations") or SASO. It is a technique used where there is no hard intelligence of weapons in the house and therefore is less intense than a normal house search. It is used in urban neighborhoods.

### Air operations

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Counterinsurgency&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Air operations")]

See also: [Counter-insurgency aircraft](/wiki/Counter-insurgency_aircraft "Counter-insurgency aircraft")

Air power can play an important role in counterinsurgency, capable of carrying out a wide range of operations:[[70]](#cite_note-70)

* Transportation in support of combatants and civilians alike, including [casualty evacuations](/wiki/Casualty_evacuation "Casualty evacuation");
* Intelligence gathering, surveillance, and reconnaissance;
* [Psychological operations](/wiki/Psychological_operation "Psychological operation"), through leaflet drops, loudspeakers, and radio broadcasts;
* Air-to-ground attack against 'soft' targets.[[71]](#cite_note-Sagraves-71)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7b/Special_Forces_Medic_in_Afghanistan_%28wide_view%29.jpg/330px-Special_Forces_Medic_in_Afghanistan_%28wide_view%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Special_Forces_Medic_in_Afghanistan_(wide_view).jpg)

A [7th SFG](/wiki/7th_Special_Forces_Group_(United_States) "7th Special Forces Group (United States)") [Special Forces](/wiki/Special_Forces_(United_States_Army) "Special Forces (United States Army)") [medic](/wiki/Combat_medic "Combat medic") gives a young boy a coloring book during a meeting with village religious leaders to gain their support and obtain information, Afghanistan 2008.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### Public diplomacy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Counterinsurgency&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Public diplomacy")]

In General [David Petraeus](/wiki/David_Petraeus "David Petraeus")' *Counterinsurgency [Field Manual](/wiki/U.S._Army_Field_Manuals "U.S. Army Field Manuals")*, one of the many tactics described to help win in counterinsurgency warfare involves the use of [public diplomacy](/wiki/Public_diplomacy "Public diplomacy") through military means.[[72]](#cite_note-72) Counterinsurgency is effective when it is integrated "into a comprehensive strategy employing all instruments of national power," including public diplomacy. The goal of COIN operations is to render the insurgents as ineffective and non-influential, by having strong and secure relations with the population of the host nation.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

An understanding of the host nation and the environment that the COIN operations will take place in is essential. Public diplomacy in COIN warfare is only effective when there is a clear understanding of the culture and population at hand. One of the largest factors needed for defeating an insurgency involves understanding the populace, how they interact with the insurgents, how they interact with [non-government organizations](/wiki/Non-government_organizations "Non-government organizations") in the area, and how they view the counterinsurgency operations themselves.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

[Ethics](/wiki/Ethics "Ethics") is a common public diplomacy aspect that is emphasized in COIN warfare. Insurgents win their war by attacking internal will and the international opposition. To combat these tactics, the counterinsurgency operations need to treat their prisoners and detainees humanely and according to American values and principles. By doing this, COIN operations show the host nation's population that they can be trusted and that they are concerned about the well-being of the population to be successful in warfare.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/62/U.S._Marine_Hearts_and_Minds.jpg/330px-U.S._Marine_Hearts_and_Minds.jpg)](/wiki/File:U.S._Marine_Hearts_and_Minds.jpg)

[Operation Moshtarak](/wiki/Operation_Moshtarak "Operation Moshtarak"); A [U.S. Marine](/wiki/U.S._Marine "U.S. Marine") hands out candy to children while conducting counterinsurgency operations in [Marjah](/wiki/Marjah "Marjah"), Afghanistan 2010.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

A population that expects the incumbent government to deliver public goods, services, and security frequently supports the counterinsurgency, and a major event that increases popular expectations of future public goods and service delivery can trigger a shift in public attitudes away from the insurgency and toward the counterinsurgency.[[73]](#cite_note-73) "Political, social, and economic programs are usually more valuable than conventional military operations in addressing the root causes of the conflict and undermining the insurgency."[[74]](#cite_note-74) These programs are essential in order to gain the support of the population. These programs are designed to make the local population feel secure, safe, and more aligned with the counterinsurgency efforts; this enables the citizens of the host nation to trust the goals and purposes of the counterinsurgency efforts, as opposed to the insurgents'. A counterinsurgency is a battle of ideas and the implementation and integration of these programs is important for success. Social, political and economic programs should be coordinated and administered by the host nation's leaders, as well. Successful COIN warfare allows the population to see that the counterinsurgency efforts are including the host nation in their re-building programs. The war is fought among the people and for the people between the insurgents and the counterinsurgents.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

A counterinsurgency is won by utilizing strategic communications and [information operations](/wiki/Information_warfare "Information warfare") successfully. A counterinsurgency is a competition of ideas, ideologies, and socio-political movements. In order to combat insurgent ideologies one must understand the values and characteristics of the [ideology](/wiki/Ideology "Ideology") or religion. Additionally, counterinsurgency efforts need to understand the culture of which the insurgency resides, in order to strategically launch information and communication operations against the insurgent ideology or religion. Counterinsurgency information operatives need to also identify key audiences, communicators, and public leaders to know whom to influence and reach out to with their information.[[75]](#cite_note-Krawchuk-75)

### Information operations

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Counterinsurgency&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Information operations")]

Public diplomacy in information operations can only be achieved by a complete understanding of the [culture](/wiki/Culture "Culture") it is operating in.
Counterinsurgency operations must be able to perceive the world from the locals' perspective. To develop a comprehensive cultural picture counterinsurgency efforts should invest in employing "media consultants, finance and business experts, psychologists, organizational network analysts, and scholars from a wide range of disciplines."[[75]](#cite_note-Krawchuk-75) Most importantly, counterinsurgency efforts need to be able to understand why the local population is drawn into the insurgent [ideology](/wiki/Ideology "Ideology"), like what aspects are appealing and how insurgents use the information to draw their followers into the ideology. Counterinsurgency communication efforts need a baseline understanding of values, attitudes, and perceptions of the people in the area of operations to conduct successful public diplomacy to defeat the enemy.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Developing information and communication strategies involve providing a legitimate alternate ideology, improving security and economic opportunity, and strengthening family ties outside of the [insurgency](/wiki/Insurgency "Insurgency"). In order to conduct public diplomacy through these means, counterinsurgency communication needs to match its deeds with its words. Information provided through public diplomacy during a counterinsurgency cannot lie, the information and communication to the people always have to be truthful and trustworthy in order to be effective at countering the insurgents. Public diplomacy in counterinsurgency to influence the public thoughts and ideas is a long time engagement and should not be done through negative campaigning about the enemy.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Conducting public diplomacy through relaying information and communicating with the public in a counterinsurgency is most successful when a conversation can happen between the counterinsurgency team and the local population of the area of operation. Building rapport with the public involves "listening, paying attention, and being responsive and proactive" which is sufficient for the local population to understand and trust the counterinsurgency efforts and vice versa.[[75]](#cite_note-Krawchuk-75) This relationship is stringent upon the counterinsurgents keeping their promises, providing security to the locals, and communicating their message directly and quickly in times of need.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Understanding and influencing the cognitive dimension of the local population is essential to winning counterinsurgency warfare. The people's perception of legitimacy about the host nation and the foreign country's counterinsurgency efforts is where success is determined. "The free flow of information present in all theaters via television, telephone, and Internet, can present conflicting messages and quickly defeat the intended effects."[[76]](#cite_note-76) Coordination between the counterinsurgency operations, the host nation, and the local media in the information presented to the public is essential to showing and influencing how the local population perceives the counterinsurgency efforts and the host nation.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

[Public opinion](/wiki/Public_opinion "Public opinion"), the media, and rumors influence how the people view counterinsurgency, the government hosting their efforts, and the host nation [legitimacy](/wiki/Legitimacy_(political) "Legitimacy (political)"). The use of public diplomacy to strategically relay the correct messages and information to the public is essential to success in a counterinsurgency operation. For example, close relationships with media members in the area is essential to ensure that the locals understand the counterinsurgency objectives and feel secure with the host nation government and the counterinsurgency efforts. If the local media is not in sync with the counterinsurgency operatives then they could spread incomplete or false information about the counterinsurgency campaign to the public.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

"Given Al Qaeda's global reach, the United States must develop a more integrated strategic communication strategy for counter-insurgency with its allies to diminish violent rhetoric, improve its image abroad, and detect, deter, and defeat this social movement at its many levels."[[75]](#cite_note-Krawchuk-75) Information operations and communicative abilities are one of the largest and most influential aspects of public diplomacy within a counterinsurgency.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Public diplomacy is especially important as modern insurgents are more easily able to gain support through a variety of sources, both local and transnational, thanks to advances in increased communication and globalization. Consequently, modern counter-insurgency requires attention to be focused on an insurgency's ecosystem from the national to the local level, in order to deprive the insurgency of support and prevent future insurgent groups from forming.[[77]](#cite_note-77)

## Specific doctrines

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Counterinsurgency&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Specific doctrines")]

### Vietnam War

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Counterinsurgency&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Vietnam War")]

During the Vietnam War, counterinsurgency initially formed part of the earlier war as Diem had implemented the poorly-conceived [Strategic Hamlet Program](/wiki/Strategic_Hamlet_Program "Strategic Hamlet Program"), a similar model to the Malayan Emergency that had the opposite effect by leading to increased recruitment to the [Viet Cong](/wiki/Viet_Cong "Viet Cong").[[78]](#cite_note-78) Similarly economic and rural development formed a key strategy as part of Rural Affairs development.[[79]](#cite_note-:0-79) While the earlier war was marked by considerable emphasis on counterinsurgency programs, the [US Armed Forces](/wiki/US_Armed_Forces "US Armed Forces") initially relied on very little, if any, theoretical doctrine of counterinsurgency during the [Ground-Intervention phase](/wiki/Joint_warfare_in_South_Vietnam,_1963%E2%80%9369#Military_strategy "Joint warfare in South Vietnam, 1963–69"). Conventional warfare using massive firepower and the failure to implement adequate counterinsurgency had extremely negative effects, which was the strategy that the NVA adeptly used to countering by the [protracted political and military warfare model](/wiki/Viet_Cong_and_PAVN_strategy,_organization_and_structure#The_Protracted_War_conflict_model "Viet Cong and PAVN strategy, organization and structure").[[79]](#cite_note-:0-79) After the replacement of General [William Westmoreland](/wiki/William_Westmoreland "William Westmoreland"), newer concepts were tried including a revival of earlier COIN strategies including [Civil Operations and Revolutionary Development Support](/wiki/Civil_Operations_and_Revolutionary_Development_Support "Civil Operations and Revolutionary Development Support"). The US and its allies later implemented the [Phoenix Program](/wiki/Phoenix_Program "Phoenix Program"), which targeted the Viet Cong political infrastructure through capture, defection or assassination of Viet Cong members. The program was very successful at suppressing Viet Cong political and revolutionary activities.[[80]](#cite_note-80)

#### Malaya

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Counterinsurgency&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Malaya")]

British forces used the relocation method with considerable success during the [Malayan Emergency](/wiki/Malayan_Emergency "Malayan Emergency"). The [Briggs Plan](/wiki/Briggs_Plan "Briggs Plan"), implemented fully in 1950, relocated [Chinese](/wiki/Malaysian_Chinese "Malaysian Chinese") [Malayans](/wiki/Federation_of_Malaya "Federation of Malaya") into protected "[New Villages](/wiki/New_village "New village")", designated by British forces. By the end of 1951, some 400,000 ethnic Chinese had moved into the fortifications. Of that population, the British forces formed a "Home Guard", an armed resistance against the [Malayan Communist Party](/wiki/Malayan_Communist_Party "Malayan Communist Party"), an implementation that was later mirrored by the [Strategic Hamlet Program](/wiki/Strategic_Hamlet_Program "Strategic Hamlet Program"), which used by US forces in [South Vietnam](/wiki/South_Vietnam "South Vietnam").[[81]](#cite_note-Nagl-81)[[82]](#cite_note-Thompson-82) Despite British claims of a victory in the Malayan Emergency, the military historian [Martin van Creveld](/wiki/Martin_van_Creveld "Martin van Creveld") noted that the results of the counterinsurgency, the withdrawal of British forces and the establishment of an independent state, are identical to those of [Aden](/wiki/Aden "Aden"), [Kenya](/wiki/Kenya "Kenya") and [Cyprus](/wiki/Cyprus "Cyprus"), which are not considered victories.[[83]](#cite_note-83)

### Dutch Empire

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Counterinsurgency&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Dutch Empire")]

The Dutch formulated a new strategy of counterinsurgency warfare during the [Aceh War](/wiki/Aceh_War "Aceh War") by deploying light-armed [Marechaussee](/wiki/Korps_Marechaussee_te_voet "Korps Marechaussee te voet") units and using [scorched-earth](/wiki/Scorched-earth "Scorched-earth") tactics.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

In 1898 [Van Heutsz](/wiki/Van_Heutsz "Van Heutsz") was proclaimed governor of Aceh, and with his lieutenant, who later became Dutch Prime Minister, [Hendrikus Colijn](/wiki/Hendrikus_Colijn "Hendrikus Colijn"), they finally conquered most of Aceh. They followed [Christiaan Snouck Hurgronje](/wiki/Christiaan_Snouck_Hurgronje "Christiaan Snouck Hurgronje")'s suggestions of finding cooperative *uleebalang* or secular chiefs to support them in the countryside and to isolate the resistance from its rural support base.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

During the [South Sulawesi Campaign](/wiki/South_Sulawesi_campaign_of_1946%E2%80%931947 "South Sulawesi campaign of 1946–1947"), Captain [Raymond Westerling](/wiki/Raymond_Westerling "Raymond Westerling") of the [KST](/wiki/Korps_Speciale_Troepen "Korps Speciale Troepen"), Special Forces of the [Royal Netherlands East Indies Army](/wiki/Royal_Netherlands_East_Indies_Army "Royal Netherlands East Indies Army") used the **Westerling Method**. Westerling ordered the registration of all [Javanese](/wiki/Javanese_people "Javanese people") arriving in [Makassar](/wiki/Makassar "Makassar") because of the large numbers of Javanese participating in the Sulawesi resistance. He also used scouts to infiltrate local villages and to identify members of the resistance.[[84]](#cite_note-84)

Based on their information and that of the Dutch military intelligence service, the DST surrounded one of more suspected villages during night and drove the population to a central location. At daybreak, the operation began, often led by Westerling. Men would be separated from women and children. From the information gathered, Westerling exposed certain people as terrorists and murderers, who were shot without any further investigation. Afterwards, Westerling forced local communities to refrain from supporting guerillas by swearing on the [Quran](/wiki/Quran "Quran"), and it established local self-defence units with some members recruited from former guerrillas deemed as "redeemable".[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Westerling directed eleven operations throughout the campaign. He succeeded in eliminating the insurgency and undermining local support for the Republicans. His actions restored Dutch rule in [southern Sulawesi](/wiki/South_Sulawesi "South Sulawesi"). However, the Netherlands East Indies government and the [Dutch Army](/wiki/Royal_Netherlands_Army "Royal Netherlands Army") command soon realised that Westerling's notoriety led to growing public criticism. In April 1947, the Dutch government instituted an official inquiry of his controversial methods. Westerling was put on the sidelines and was relieved of his duties in November 1948.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### France

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Counterinsurgency&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: France")]

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
|  | This article's **tone or style may not reflect the [encyclopedic tone](/wiki/Wikipedia:Writing_better_articles#Tone "Wikipedia:Writing better articles") used on Wikipedia**. See Wikipedia's [guide to writing better articles](/wiki/Wikipedia:Writing_better_articles#Tone "Wikipedia:Writing better articles") for suggestions. *(February 2020)* *([Learn how and when to remove this message](/wiki/Help:Maintenance_template_removal "Help:Maintenance template removal"))* |

France had major counterinsurgency wars in its [colonies](/wiki/Colonies "Colonies") in [Indochina](/wiki/Indochina "Indochina") and [Algeria](/wiki/Algeria "Algeria"). McClintock cited the basic points of French doctrine as:[[85]](#cite_note-McClintock-2005-85)

* Quadrillage (an administrative grid of population and territory)
* Ratissage (cordoning and "raking")
* Regroupement (relocating and closely controlling a suspect population)
* 'Tache d'huile' – The 'oil spot' strategy
* Recruitment of local leaders and forces
* Paramilitary organization and militias

Much of the thinking was informed by the work of earlier leading French theoreticians of colonial warfare and counterinsurgency, Marshals Bugeaud, Gallieni and Lyautey.[[63]](#cite_note-porch-63)

WMcClintock cites the 1894 Algerian governor, [Jules Cambon](/wiki/Jules_Cambon "Jules Cambon"), as saying, "By destroying the administration and local government we were also suppressing our means of action.... The result is that we are today confronted by a sort of human dust on which we have no influence and in which movements take place which are unknown to us". Cambon's philosophy, however, did not seem to survive into the [Algerian War of Independence](/wiki/Algerian_War_of_Independence "Algerian War of Independence")(1954–1962).[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/5/57/Dautranh.jpg)](/wiki/File:Dautranh.jpg)

The sophisticated NLF/PAVN insurgent structure was embedded in the population and difficult to root out and combat. French and US Special Forces (Green Berets) saw some success in organizing tribal peoples to fight.[[86]](#cite_note-86)

#### Indochina

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Counterinsurgency&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: Indochina")]

Post-war doctrine, as in [Indochina](/wiki/French_Indochina "French Indochina"), took a more drastic view of "*Guerre Révolutionnaire*", which presented an ideological and global war with a commitment to [total war](/wiki/Total_war "Total war"). Countermeasures, in principle, needed to be both political and military: "No measure was too drastic to meet the new threat of revolution". French forces taking control from the [Japanese](/wiki/Japan "Japan") did not seem to negotiate seriously with nationalist elements in what was to become [Vietnam](/wiki/Vietnam "Vietnam"),[[87]](#cite_note-87) which reaped the consequences of overconfidence at [Điện Biên Phủ](/wiki/%C4%90i%E1%BB%87n_Bi%C3%AAn_Ph%E1%BB%A7 "Điện Biên Phủ").[[88]](#cite_note-Fall-2002-88)

It occurred to various commanders that soldiers who were trained to operate as guerrillas would have a strong sense of how to fight guerrillas. Before the partition of [French Indochina](/wiki/French_Indochina "French Indochina"), French *[Groupement de Commandos Mixtes Aéroportés](/wiki/Groupement_de_Commandos_Mixtes_A%C3%A9roport%C3%A9s "Groupement de Commandos Mixtes Aéroportés")* (GCMA), led by [Roger Trinquier](/wiki/Roger_Trinquier "Roger Trinquier"),[[89]](#cite_note-Trinquier-89) took on this role, drawing on French experience with the Jedburgh teams.[[90]](#cite_note-90) GCMA, operating in [Tonkin](/wiki/Tonkin "Tonkin") and [Laos](/wiki/Laos "Laos") under [French intelligence](/wiki/French_intelligence "French intelligence"), was complemented by Commandos Nord Viêt-Nam in the North. In those missions, the SOF teams lived and fought with the locals. One Laotian, who became an officer, was [Vang Pao](/wiki/Vang_Pao "Vang Pao"), who was to become a general in Hmong and Laotian operations in [Southeast Asia](/wiki/Southeast_Asia "Southeast Asia") while the US forces increased their role.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

#### Algeria

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Counterinsurgency&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: Algeria")]

Further information: [Algerian War](/wiki/Algerian_War "Algerian War")

The French counterinsurgency in [colonial Algeria](/wiki/French_Algeria "French Algeria") was a savage one. The 1957 [Battle of Algiers](/wiki/Battle_of_Algiers_(1957) "Battle of Algiers (1957)") resulted in 24,000 detentions, with most tortured and an estimated 3,000 killed. It may have broken the [National Liberation Front](/wiki/National_Liberation_Front_(Algeria) "National Liberation Front (Algeria)") infrastructure in [Algiers](/wiki/Algiers "Algiers"), but it also killed off French legitimacy as far as "[hearts and minds](/wiki/Winning_hearts_and_minds "Winning hearts and minds")" went.[[85]](#cite_note-McClintock-2005-85)[[91]](#cite_note-Tomes2004-91)

Counterinsurgency requires an extremely capable intelligence infrastructure that is endowed with human sources and deep cultural knowledge. That contributes to the difficulty of foreign, as opposed to indigenous, powers in counterinsurgency operations.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

One of France's most influential theorists was [Roger Trinquier](/wiki/Roger_Trinquier "Roger Trinquier"). The *Modern Warfare* counterinsurgency strategy, described by Trinquier, who had led [anti-communist](/wiki/Anti-communist "Anti-communist") guerrillas in Indochina, was a strong influence on French efforts in Algeria.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Trinquier suggested three principles:

1. separate the guerrilla from the population that supports it;
2. occupy the zones from which the guerrillas previously operated, make the area dangerous for the insurgents and turn the people against the guerrilla movement; and
3. co-ordinate actions over a wide area and for a long enough time that the guerrilla is denied access to the population centres that could support him[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Trinquier's view was that [torture](/wiki/Torture "Torture") had to be extremely focused and limited, but many French officers considered its use to be corrosive to their own side.[[92]](#cite_note-92) There were strong protests among French leaders: the Army's most decorated officer, General [Jacques Pâris de Bollardière](/wiki/Jacques_P%C3%A2ris_de_Bollardi%C3%A8re "Jacques Pâris de Bollardière"), confronted General [Jacques Massu](/wiki/Jacques_Massu "Jacques Massu"), the commander of French forces in the Battle of Algiers, over orders institutionalizing torture, as "an unleashing of deplorable instincts which no longer knew any limits". He issued an open letter condemning the danger to the army of the loss of its moral values "under the fallacious pretext of immediate expediency" and was imprisoned for sixty days.[[85]](#cite_note-McClintock-2005-85)

As some of the French Army protested, others increased the intensity of their approach, which led to an attempted [military coup](/wiki/Military_coup "Military coup") against the [French Fourth Republic](/wiki/French_Fourth_Republic "French Fourth Republic") itself. Massu and General [Raoul Salan](/wiki/Raoul_Salan "Raoul Salan") led a 1958 coup in [Algiers](/wiki/Algiers "Algiers") to demand a new republic under [Charles de Gaulle](/wiki/Charles_de_Gaulle "Charles de Gaulle"). When de Gaulle's policies toward Algeria, such as a 1961 referendum on Algerian self-determination, did not meet the expectations of the colonial officers, Salan formed the underground [Organisation armée secrète](/wiki/Organisation_arm%C3%A9e_secr%C3%A8te "Organisation armée secrète") (OAS), a [right-wing](/wiki/Right-wing "Right-wing") terrorist group, whose actions included a 1962 assassination attempt against de Gaulle himself.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

#### West Africa

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Counterinsurgency&action=edit&section=29 "Edit section: West Africa")]

France has had taken [Barnett's Leviathan role](/wiki/Insurgency#Barnett_and_connecting_to_the_core "Insurgency")[[93]](#cite_note-Barnett-93) in [Chad](/wiki/Chad "Chad") and [Ivory Coast](/wiki/Ivory_Coast "Ivory Coast"), the latter on two occasions, most significantly in 2002–2003.[[94]](#cite_note-Ivory2003-94) The situation with France and Ivory Coast is not a classic FID situation, as [France attacked Ivorian forces](/wiki/Ivorian-French_War "Ivorian-French War") that had provoked [UN peacekeepers](/wiki/UN_peacekeepers "UN peacekeepers").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Another noteworthy instance of counterinsurgency in [West Africa](/wiki/West_Africa "West Africa") is the [Nigerian Armed Forces](/wiki/Nigerian_Armed_Forces "Nigerian Armed Forces") experience against the [Boko Haram insurgency](/wiki/Boko_Haram_insurgency "Boko Haram insurgency"). Military operations against [Boko Haram](/wiki/Boko_Haram "Boko Haram") occur predominantly in the far northeast areas of Nigeria. The operations have been ongoing since June 2011 and have greatly expanded within the [Lake Chad Basin](/wiki/Chad_Basin "Chad Basin") sub-region of West Africa.[[95]](#cite_note-95)

### India

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Counterinsurgency&action=edit&section=30 "Edit section: India")]

There have been many insurgencies in [India](/wiki/India "India") since its [independence](/wiki/Indian_independence_movement "Indian independence movement") in 1947. The [Kashmir insurgency](/wiki/Insurgency_in_Jammu_and_Kashmir "Insurgency in Jammu and Kashmir"), which started by 1989, was brought under control by the Indian government and violence has been reduced. A branch of the [Indian Army](/wiki/Indian_Army "Indian Army"), known as the [Rashtriya Rifles](/wiki/Rashtriya_Rifles "Rashtriya Rifles") (RR), was created for the sole purpose of destroying the insurgency in Kashmir, and it has played a major role in doing so. The RR was well supported by [Central Reserve Police Force](/wiki/Central_Reserve_Police_Force "Central Reserve Police Force") (CRPF), [Border Security Force](/wiki/Border_Security_Force "Border Security Force") (BSF), [Indo-Tibetan Border Police](/wiki/Indo-Tibetan_Border_Police "Indo-Tibetan Border Police") (ITBP) and state government police.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The [Counter Insurgency and Jungle Warfare School](/wiki/Counter_Insurgency_and_Jungle_Warfare_School "Counter Insurgency and Jungle Warfare School") (CIJWS) is located in the northeastern town of [Vairengte](/wiki/Vairengte "Vairengte") in the Indian [state](/wiki/States_of_India "States of India") of [Mizoram](/wiki/Mizoram "Mizoram"). Personnel from countries such as the [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States"), the [United Kingdom](/wiki/United_Kingdom "United Kingdom"), [France](/wiki/France "France"), [Russia](/wiki/Russia "Russia"), [Kazakhstan](/wiki/Kazakhstan "Kazakhstan"), [Tajikistan](/wiki/Tajikistan "Tajikistan"), [Bangladesh](/wiki/Bangladesh "Bangladesh") and [Vietnam](/wiki/Vietnam "Vietnam") have attended this school.[[96]](#cite_note-Dawn-96)

### Portugal

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Counterinsurgency&action=edit&section=31 "Edit section: Portugal")]

Main article: [Portuguese Colonial War](/wiki/Portuguese_Colonial_War "Portuguese Colonial War")

[Portugal](/wiki/Portugal "Portugal")'s experience in counterinsurgency resulted from the "pacification" campaigns conducted in the [Portuguese African and Asian colonies](/wiki/Portuguese_Empire "Portuguese Empire") in the late 19th century and the early 20th century.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

In the 1960s and the early 1970s, Portugal conducted large-scale counterinsurgency operations in [Angola](/wiki/Portuguese_Angola "Portuguese Angola"), [Portuguese Guinea](/wiki/Portuguese_Guinea "Portuguese Guinea") and [Mozambique](/wiki/Portuguese_Mozambique "Portuguese Mozambique") against independentist guerrillas supported by the [Eastern Bloc](/wiki/Eastern_Bloc "Eastern Bloc") and [China](/wiki/China "China"), as well by some [Western countries](/wiki/Western_World "Western World"). Although the campaigns are collectively known as the "[Portuguese Colonial War](/wiki/Portuguese_Colonial_War "Portuguese Colonial War")", there were in fact three different ones: the [Angolan Independence War](/wiki/Angolan_Independence_War "Angolan Independence War"), the [Guinea-Bissau War of Independence](/wiki/Guinea-Bissau_War_of_Independence "Guinea-Bissau War of Independence") and the [Mozambican War of Independence](/wiki/Mozambican_War_of_Independence "Mozambican War of Independence"). The situation was unique in that small armed forces, [those of Portugal](/wiki/Portuguese_Armed_Forces "Portuguese Armed Forces"), conducted three counterinsurgency wars at the same time in three different [theatres of operations](/wiki/Theatre_of_operations "Theatre of operations") separated by thousands of [kilometres](/wiki/Kilometres "Kilometres"). For those operations, Portugal developed its own counterinsurgency doctrine.[[97]](#cite_note-97)

### Russia and Soviet Union

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Counterinsurgency&action=edit&section=32 "Edit section: Russia and Soviet Union")]

The most familiar Russian counterinsurgency is the [War in Afghanistan](/wiki/Soviet%E2%80%93Afghan_War "Soviet–Afghan War") from 1979 to 1989. However, throughout the history of the [Russian Empire](/wiki/Russian_Empire "Russian Empire"), the Russians fought many counterinsurgencies as new [Caucasian](/wiki/Russian_conquest_of_the_Caucasus "Russian conquest of the Caucasus") and [Central Asian territory were occupied](/wiki/Russian_conquest_of_Central_Asia "Russian conquest of Central Asia").[[98]](#cite_note-auto-98) It was in those conflicts that the Russians developed the following counterinsurgency tactics:[[98]](#cite_note-auto-98)

1. Deploy a significant number of troops
2. Isolate the area from outside assistance
3. Establish tight control of major cities and towns
4. Build lines of forts to restrict insurgent movement
5. Destroy the springs of resistance through destruction of settlements, livestock, crops etc.

Those tactics, generally speaking, were carried over into Soviet use following the 1917 revolution for the most part except for the integration of political-military command.[[99]](#cite_note-99) This tactical blueprint saw use following the [First](/wiki/World_War_I "World War I") and [Second World Wars](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II") in Dagestan, the Caucasus, Central Asia, Siberia, Lithuania and Ukraine.[[98]](#cite_note-auto-98) That doctrine was ultimately shown to be inadequate in the Soviet War in Afghanistan, mostly because of insufficient troop commitment, and in the Wars in Chechnya.[[98]](#cite_note-auto-98)

### United States

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Counterinsurgency&action=edit&section=33 "Edit section: United States")]

Main article: [Security force assistance](/wiki/Security_force_assistance "Security force assistance")

The United States has conducted counterinsurgency campaigns during the [Philippine–American War](/wiki/Philippine%E2%80%93American_War "Philippine–American War"), the Vietnam War, the post-2001 [War in Afghanistan](/wiki/War_in_Afghanistan_(2001%E2%80%93present) "War in Afghanistan (2001–present)"), and the [Iraq War](/wiki/Iraq_War "Iraq War"). The wars in Iraq and Afghanistan have resulted in increased interest in counterinsurgency within the American military, which was exemplified by the 2006 publication of a new joint Army [Field Manual](/wiki/United_States_Army_Field_Manuals "United States Army Field Manuals") 3-24/Marine Corps Warfighting Publication No. 3-33.5, *Counterinsurgency*, which replaced the documents separately published by the Army and Marine Corps 20–25 years prior.[[100]](#cite_note-100) Views of the doctrine contained in the manual have been mixed.[[101]](#cite_note-101) The 2014 version of FM 3-24/MCWP 3–33.5 acquired a new title, *Insurgencies and Countering Insurgencies*, it consists of three main parts,

> Part one provides strategic and operational context, part two provides the doctrine for understanding insurgencies, and part three provides doctrine for defeating an insurgency. In short, FM 3-24/MCWP 3–33.5 is organized to provide the context of a problem, the problem, and possible solutions.[[102]](#cite_note-102)

[William B. Caldwell IV](/wiki/William_B._Caldwell_IV "William B. Caldwell IV") wrote:

> The [law of armed conflict](/wiki/Law_of_armed_conflict "Law of armed conflict") requires that, to use force, "[combatants](/wiki/Combatant "Combatant")" must distinguish individuals presenting a threat from innocent [civilians](/wiki/Civilian "Civilian"). This basic principle is accepted by all disciplined militaries. In the counterinsurgency, disciplined application of force is even more critical because our enemies camouflage themselves in the civilian population. Our success in Iraq depends on our ability to treat the civilian population with humanity and dignity, even as we remain ready to immediately defend ourselves or Iraqi civilians when a threat is detected.[[103]](#cite_note-Caldwell-103)

In the recent conflicts the [101st Airborne Division](/wiki/101st_Airborne_Division "101st Airborne Division") (Air Assault) has been increasingly involved training and developing allied military and security forces.[[104]](#cite_note-104)[[105]](#cite_note-stripes.com-105) When done by major maneouvre forces, this is known as [security force assistance](/wiki/Security_force_assistance "Security force assistance"). It was announced 14 January 2016 that 1,800 soldiers from the 101st's Headquarters and its 2nd Brigade Combat Team will deploy soon on regular rotations to [Baghdad](/wiki/Baghdad "Baghdad") and [Erbil](/wiki/Erbil "Erbil") to train and advise [Iraqi Army](/wiki/Iraqi_Army "Iraqi Army") and [Kurdish Peshmerga](/wiki/Peshmerga "Peshmerga") forces who are expected in the coming months to move toward [Mosul](/wiki/Mosul "Mosul"), the [Islamic State](/wiki/Islamic_State "Islamic State")'s de facto headquarters in [Iraq](/wiki/Iraq "Iraq").[[106]](#cite_note-106)[*[unreliable source?](/wiki/Wikipedia:Reliable_sources "Wikipedia:Reliable sources")*]

The 101st Airborne Division will serve an integral role in preparing Iraqi ground troops to expel the Islamic State group from Mosul, Defense Secretary [Ash Carter](/wiki/Ash_Carter "Ash Carter") told the division's soldiers during a January 2016 visit to [Fort Campbell](/wiki/Fort_Campbell "Fort Campbell"), [Kentucky](/wiki/Kentucky "Kentucky").[[105]](#cite_note-stripes.com-105) Defense Secretary Ash Carter told the 101st Airborne Division that "The Iraqi and Peshmerga forces you will train, advise and assist have proven their determination, their resiliency, and increasingly, their capability, but they need you to continue building on that success, preparing them for the fight today and the long hard fight for their future. They need your skill. They need your experience."[[105]](#cite_note-stripes.com-105)

Foreign internal defense policymaking has subsequently aided in Iraqi successes in [reclaiming Tikrit](/wiki/Second_Battle_of_Tikrit "Second Battle of Tikrit"), [Baiji](/wiki/Battle_of_Baiji_(2014%E2%80%9315) "Battle of Baiji (2014–15)"), [Ramadi](/wiki/Battle_of_Ramadi_(2015%E2%80%9316) "Battle of Ramadi (2015–16)"), [Fallujah](/wiki/Battle_of_Fallujah_(2016) "Battle of Fallujah (2016)"), and [Mosul](/wiki/Battle_of_Mosul_(2016%E2%80%9317) "Battle of Mosul (2016–17)") from the [Islamic State of Iraq and the Levant](/wiki/Islamic_State_of_Iraq_and_the_Levant "Islamic State of Iraq and the Levant").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Recent evaluations of U.S. counterinsurgency efforts in Afghanistan have yielded mixed results. A comprehensive study by the [Special Inspector General for Afghanistan Reconstruction](/wiki/Special_Inspector_General_for_Afghanistan_Reconstruction "Special Inspector General for Afghanistan Reconstruction") concluded that "the U.S. government greatly overestimated its ability" to use COIN and stabilization tactics for long-term success.[[107]](#cite_note-SIGAR-107) The report found that "successes in stabilizing Afghan districts rarely lasted longer than the physical presence of coalition troops and civilians." These findings are corroborated by scholarly studies of U.S. counterinsurgency activities in Afghanistan, which determined that backlashes by insurgents and the local population were common.[[108]](#cite_note-108)[[109]](#cite_note-109)[[110]](#cite_note-110)
